sentences.text: Can, can I go back still a--
I just wanted to mention-- when he arrived in Gross Rosen, we still had some-- our personal, personal things with us.
And when we arrived there, we had to undress and go through a bath, you know.
And we came out from the other side.
And down there, they gave us, you know, different clothes.
And one thing what I want to mention-- I still had, till Gross Rosen, end of '44, my tefillin with me.
And that's what I had to-- even I, I couldn't use them.
But I had them with me.
That's when I had to part with them.
And that's where we got different, different clothes over there.
Well, some people that used them, you know, they get up early in the morning, and between the, the, the beds, you know, they used to put them on, you know.
I did not use them.
But I used to carry them all the time till, till the end of '44.
They, they meant a lot, you know, because once you become bar mitzvah, that's, that's when you become a man and a Jew, you know.
And I use them now almost every day because I go very often to temple.
No.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You could say it in, in-- in silence, you could pray, you know.
From the time which you got up in the morning or till they chase you out from the barracks.
Well, the religious Jews, mostly they knew it, you know, because I imagine they figure out from the Jewish calendars, you know.
They didn't have the calendars.
But in their heads, you know, they, they, they knew it when it comes out, this.
I remember once, you know, when it was Rosh Hashanah, New Year's-- Jewish New Year's.
So they prayed louder, you know, than usual, you know.
No, because that was in, in personal barrack, you know.

That was-- in the barracks, you didn't have no, no SS men or-- but sometimes you, you, you can have trouble not only from the-- from the Germans, but from Jewish-- how do you call it-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the head of the barrack, you know.
I got once 15 lashes on my-- on my behind from our-- from my Jewish head, head of, of the barrack because he said I didn't make the bed good.
No, I don't think so.
I don't think so because they-- usually, they-- Plaszow was-- they enlarged.
There were coming more and more people in.
And there was something else.
They had a camp inside Plaszow for Poles, too.
You know, when, when-- what they caught them for stealing or something.
But they, they were there.
They were sentenced for six months or a year or three months.
But they didn't mingle with us together.
They really-- they had to stay in the barracks.
They couldn't-- they couldn't walk around or, or, or do any work, you know.
Yes, we arrived in Brunnlitz.
It was very cold.
I think was wintertime.
And he took over a, a factory.
What they used to do before, they used to manufacture sweaters there.
So we took apart the, the machines.
And we started to build a new factory to-- for ammunition.
And by that time, they brought 300 women to the camp.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the beginning, I was working in the inside in the-- in the factory, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] doing all kinds of things with machines.
And, and then, then they sent me to work outside.
I was the one who was building a-- for Schindler the [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] stable.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it took quite some time, you know, [LAUGHS] to finish that stable, you know.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember one time-- we didn't have too much to eat by Schindler either because he couldn't-- he couldn't do nothing about food either, you know.
So I remember one time-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there were tracks not far from us.
They were railroad tracks.
And they brought, I don't know, two or three wagons.
And we didn't know what's, what's in that-- wagons?
And it took some times to open the wagons because they were so frozen.
And there were people inside.
Half of them were frozen to death.
And, and they were Hungarian Jews.
And Schindler took him-- took them to his factory, the inside.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He gave them heated barracks.
They didn't have to work.
He gave them more food than he got.
And he brought them back to, to, to, to life, you know.
He brought them back to, to, to be a human.
But the problem was that a lot of them were-- still were dying, a lot of them.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Because, you see, Hungarian Jews, they-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the hunger was killing them more than a Polish Jew.
You see, the Polish Jews were more hardened, hardened, you know.
They could-- they could last longer without food on-- and they couldn't.
So they were-- I remember they were walking, you know, on the-- whatever, all kind of bones from, from, from, from, from food or something, you know.
And they were picking them up and licking or eating, you know.
And, and they die naturally, a lot of them, you know.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They, they, they were-- couldn't take hunger as, as good as the Polish Jews.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
On the-- that's how we worked it in 1945.
It came by, by 10 day before-- I think it was-- must be the beginning of May or end of April.
He calls all the people, Schindler, in the-- in the factory.
And he told them the world is coming to a end.
You don't have to work anymore.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he said, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] don't, don't take your law in your own hands against the, the, the people who were guarding you.
They're going to be-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] go through courts, you know, like [?
Berlini. ?] But before that, you see, there was in, in Czechoslovakia, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a big Czech Resistance.
And they were keeping an eye on us.
Because this Schindler, our, our SS man-- there was a, a a, a, a man by the name SS Obersturmfuhrer Leopold.
He was a young guy.
He was the commander of, of the SS, of our camp.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And Schindler sent him to the front.
He said, you are a young fellow.
You should go fight for the-- for the Fatherland.
He mean for your-- for your country.
And he sent out all the-- all the SS mens.
They came together.
And they brought in, you know, old soldiers, you know.
They, they were SS, too, but [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] older one, not, not younger one, you know.
And that's-- they were much, much-- well, we didn't have no problem with the-- even with the other one-- SS man.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
A, a what?
Where you make flours for baking, you know.
Mill, yeah, yeah.
And you know, they always ask for people to that mill.
And they pay for it with flour.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And the-- and the people who went to work there-- I never went.
They used to come.
We used to have, you know, in, in their pants, you know, flour, you know, so they could, could make something-- to cook something to eat.
But after the war, we find out that it was a woman owner of that mill, she was Jewish.
And she didn't need the people to go work there.
But she just wanted to help out, you know.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And by the end of the war-- that was around, around April-- he used to send out his trucks, Schindler, to raid other factories, other German factories.
He used to empty them and bring them into our camp.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Everybody who left from Schindler got a package of, of materials, all kind of materials, you know.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Like, I mean, materials for, for suit, for a coat, or for a shirt, you know.
A lot people throw them away because you couldn't have strength to carry it, you know.
But I did brought them, I remember, to, to Poland, I think.
And we were liberated May the 8th, 12 o'clock at midnight.
And then we were for two days alone.
The-- till the Russian came the 10th of, of May.
So we were alone for two days.
But Schindler left-- I remember he left May the 8th exact at midnight.
I don't know.
We were bewildered because you don't know where you are, what to do, how-- where to go.
And I remember we just walked.
And we were walking.
We didn't know where we going.
You were walking.
And that's when I had that friend-- when I had a very close friend.
He is now in Israel.
We used to sleep together.
But we, we met together by Schindler still in, in, in Emalia in, in Cracow.
And from that time, we were all together.
And we left together.
We didn't know where we going.
We were going because this-- the roads are full of people.
So everybody goes one way.
And we were going the same way.
But where we were going, we didn't know it.
His name is a Border, Markus Broder.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were all the time together.
He was a, a couple years older from me.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we got to-- we got to a railroad station.
We boarded a, a train.
But we couldn't go inside.
So we, we sit on the-- on the roof of the train.
We didn't know where that train is going.
We're just going, you know, wherever it goes, you know.
Who cares where it goes?
And that's how we-- just happened that we arrived to Prague, Czechoslovakia.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] These people were excellent, you know.
They were such a nice people, the Czechs.
There is no other people-- I remember you walked in, in a bakery.
They didn't ask you nothing.
They give you a loaf of bread.
They didn't want no money.
You went on the-- on the streetcar-- and we were always looking for where is our relief organization.
I'll never forget-- Get the conductor stopped, stopped the train.
And he went out with us.
You see, you go there.
Make a right.
Make a left.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And by the way, the Czech Resistance, when we were in camp, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they, they was-- plans to send us away from, from, from, from Brunnlitz, from Schindler.
But that night, the Czech Resistance surrounded the camp.
In other words, if there would be something happen, they would help us whoever they could to escape-- with escape, you know.
But meanwhile, Schindler sent away, you know, all the, the big shot, the big SS from-- he send them on the front.
That way Schindler had a lot power an Czechoslovakia.
And don't forget one thing.
He had to do something for the German to, to get all that, you know.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]
So [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] then from Prague, we boarded, I think, another train that we knew already is going to the Polish border.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then we, we took that train.
I remember everything on the-- on the roof of the thing because there was never room on inside.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we arrived on the border.
And we went to Cracow.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And when we got to Cracow, there was a, a street what they called Dluga, you know.
And that's where it was a Jewish committee who was registering people coming from camps.
But then, when we got there, we found out that the Polish Army is, is taking the, the Jews who are coming from concentration camp to the army, to the Polish Army.
So when I was there, I didn't want to register.
And so then it-- I coming from a concentration camp, and I'm going to go to the Polish Army?
So I didn't leave my name there.
And a lot-- I have a lot of friends who, who went to the Polish Army to-- for, for two years.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we stayed in-- we stayed in Poland.
We slept every night someplace else, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know, a few weeks.
And we got together-- a few friends together.
And we're going to Israel.
Because-- listen, I came back.
I was in Cracow.
I went back to Bochnia.
And I found out nobody's there.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I was alone-- nobody.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And in '45, when the war ended, I wasn't yet 20 years old.
I was 19 and 1/2 years old.
And as we were traveling, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were-- we wind up in Austria.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we met that other friend.
And we stayed there in Austria in a DP camp.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And then we moved to another camp in Austria, too.
That was Linz, Austria.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But they call that camp-- it was a very big camp called by the name Bindermichl.
Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] wasn't too bad.
First of all, we had-- we had very nice apartments there.
Because we wind up there-- that used to be three-story houses built during the war for, for Hermann-Goering Werke.
But, I mean, you know, they were fabrics on his-- under his name.
And they built for the workers these apartments.
And then after the war, we stay in this apartment-- beautiful apartments, can't complain.
So we had, like, a three-bedroom apartment.
Then we were there together about six or seven-- we were together, girls and, and boys, you know.
And there was UNRRA at that time, you know.
And we got rations from the UNRRA.
And you met people, you know, what, what-- from, from camps where you were and from other camps.
There were a lot people from, from Schindler there in that camp, too.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in 1946, I met one of the girls what we used to live together.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And you-- I had an uncle-- I think I mentioned-- who used to live in Brazil, in Rio de Janeiro.
And I, I remember his address by heart.
And, you know, they used to come every day and-- asking, you want to send a telegram someplace, or whatever?
I said, yes.
That's nice.
I figured I wasn't sure if that telegram is going to get someplace.
So I knew his address.
So I gave the address.
And after a few weeks, I got a letter from him that he wants to bring me over to Brazil.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] So and except that, I had-- you know, every day, were coming from different country, from South America, from the United States.
So I just-- whenever they came, I register, you know, to go to the United States.
I registered to go to Canada, to South America.
Wherever anybody ask me if you want to go someplace, I registered.
Because I figured I have nobody no-- nobody no place anyway.
So--
So the first thing came that I can go to Brazil.
So I remember the night before we left, we, we got married in, in a-- in that camp in Linz.
My wife's name was Stephanie-- Stephanie [?
Feuerstein. ?] She is not alive.
Not-- that that's my second wife.
And, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and they told us there was no consulate, Brazilian consulate, in, in Austria at that time.
They told us we have to go to Paris to get the visa.
So we started, and we arrived in Germany, in Munich.
And we stayed there for six weeks in a camp, too.
And then they took us by, by-- not by-- not by train, but by trucks to, to Paris.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we arrived in Paris.
And the Joint Distribution Committee set us up in, in, in hotels.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we find out that the Brazilian doesn't want to accept Jews.
They don't want to let in Jews.
So we couldn't get visas.
So we stayed there in, in Paris.
And after a few years, there was a Polish priest who used to give papers that you are not-- Aryan.
But I didn't want to take.
If I cannot go as a Jew, I don't want to go.
Then, later on, they did want to allow the Jews.
But I didn't want to go anymore.
And that's how I stayed in Paris for 10 years.
In Paris, I was a tailor.
Yes, my two children were born in Paris.
One was born in '48 and my daughter in '54.
My son's name is Jack.
And my daughter's name is Lillian.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] being in Paris for, for 10 years, suddenly, I get a letter from-- to show up on the CIA for, for interview.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I got there, you know.
I meet their people, you know, because they do that-- before, you know, they let you go to the United States, they interview you.
So the people there, I see-- where are you going?
I have a brother.
I have a uncle.
I have a aunt.
They ask me, who is your sponsor?
I said, I don't know.
I have no idea.
I, I did remember I registered, you know-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 12, 13 years ago, I registered to go to the United States.
But I didn't have nobody here.
But they still interviewed me, you know.
And, and a few months later, I-- they send me the papers to go to the United States.
And that's how I came here in 1956-- in January of 1956.
And I came here with the biggest ships in the world.
It was the The ship at that time was less than a year old.
What?
You don't remember that.
The ship is still in, in the-- in the west or someplace--
--as a tourist attraction.
Well, you see, my wife-- first wife, she had a cousin here.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But the papers-- the Joint Distribution Committee, they, they got the papers from a-- from a company, you know, that they-- in other words, when you get here, they're going to give me job.
In other words, I'm not on the mercy of the-- of the government.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I remember that cousin even awaited us on the-- by the ship when we arrived.
But we went to HIAS.
At that time it was HIAS.
And we stayed in HIAS for, for a few weeks till we found an apartment.
And that was the first apartment we lived in-- the Bronx.
No.
I didn't speak-- I didn't speak English.
And I didn't speak French when I got to Paris.
In Paris, I went-- I went in evening schools for a while.
Here, I didn't go to schools, no.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's why I still don't speak too good English.
[LAUGHS] Got the-- you learn at work, you know.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] We lived in the Bronx.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I remember I got that job in the beginning as a presser, you know, in a-- in a-- in a-- by a tailor.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I didn't like the job, you know.
It was a very hard job, you know.
It was-- then I found a job by a diamond company.
The company's name was Clipper Diamond Tool.
And that's where I worked for over 30 years in that company [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] till I retired.
And in-between, my wife passed away in 1973.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She passed, you know, with much worse camps than I did.
She lost a kidney.
And I imagine that was her problem.
She died just suddenly.
She went to sleep at night.
In the morning, she was gone.
And she didn't have no heart attack.
